summary: Gruber provides a detailed comparison of the newly announced MacBook Air against the standard MacBook and MacBook Pro. He methodically lists the Air's advantages (thinness, weight, LED display, backlit keyboard, multi-touch trackpad) and disadvantages (slower CPU, less storage, limited RAM, no optical drive, sealed battery, fewer ports). He concludes the Air is clearly designed as a secondary machine rather than a primary computer. He personally would have preferred a smaller footprint akin to the 12-inch PowerBook G4, and predicts the Air will be a hit, though perhaps not a huge one.
key_points: The MacBook Air's main appeal is size and weight (3 lbs vs 5 lbs), but it trades off significantly in performance, storage, and expandability
The Air's sealed battery will draw heavy press criticism but most users won't care — mirroring the iPhone battery controversy
The lack of FireWire, single USB port, and no optical drive firmly position the Air as a secondary machine alongside a desktop Mac
Gruber would have preferred a smaller 12-inch footprint rather than a thinner version of the same 13.3-inch form factor
The 2 GB RAM ceiling is a meaningful limitation — swapping to disk when memory is insufficient will seriously hurt performance
money_quotes: Clearly, the main appeal of the MacBook Air is its size and weight, and the main trade-off is performance and storage capacity.
The Air is almost half the weight of a 15-inch MacBook Pro (5.4 lbs.) For many people, that's all she wrote, and on this basis alone they've already pre-ordered an Air.

I think this aspect is going to play out exactly as it has with the iPhone: (a) there's going to be a ton of criticism from the gadget and tech press decrying this as a terrible decision; and (b) the vast majority of users don't care and won't mind the sealed battery at all.
Seriously, the press is not going to shut up about this — you can't go 10 feet in the Expo media room without hearing someone complain about it.
I'd have rather seen a smaller footprint, a la the old 12-inch PowerBook G4 — something just exactly as wide as a full keyboard.
The MacBook Air is undeniably beautiful and clever, but clearly designed as a secondary machine, not a main machine.
key_insight: The MacBook Air is an undeniably beautiful secondary machine, but its trade-offs in performance, storage, and expandability make it a poor choice as anyone's sole computer.
